text: So here's a sequence of steps I recommend for a left lower lobectomy.
In this case I've chosen 13 discrete steps, but of course this is completely arbitrary.
But the point I want to make is the first sequence of steps, in my case the first six steps, I do the same in every case.
It's essentially the hilar dissection, the lymph node dissection.
It sets the lobectomy up.

The second sequence of steps, in this case the last seven steps, can, and I recommend the surgeon should do them in whatever order is necessary based on the patient's anatomy, whether the fissure is thick or there's aberrant vascular anatomy.
The surgeon should have the flexibility to do the last sequence of steps in whatever way is most efficient for that lobectomy.
But for an ideal case, as this one is, the first step is to lift the lung up,
Dissect the inferior pulmonary ligament.
Then isolate the inferior pulmonary vein.
Then the lung is pushed anterior and the posterior hilum is dissected, particularly the station 7 or subcarinal lymph nodes.
Dissect out the posterior bronchus, the posterior pulmonary artery.
Then the lung is reflected inferior and the superior hilum is dissected and the station 5 and 6 lymph nodes are dissected out.
Then the lung is pushed posterior.
like a vats retraction and the anterior hilum is dissected, particularly the station 10 lymph nodes between the upper and lower lobe bronchus.
And then I'm set up for the lobectomy.
The first thing I always do is I look at the fissure.
If it's a good fissure, I want to expose the PA within the fissure.
And then hopefully I can divide the anterior fissure, which would expose the basilar pulmonary artery trunk, which I can divide.
And this will
then expose the superior segmental artery, which is divided next.
Then usually I divide the inferior pulmonary vein and the posterior fissure.
And the last thing I do is divide the left lower lobe bronchus, thus detaching the lobe.
But again, that's an ideal situation.
And the second sequence of steps can be done in any order based upon the patient's anatomy.
So here's the robotic left lower lobectomy.

Note that I use these Kittner sponge rolls, we call them cigarettes, to push the lobe rather than grab it with the forceps because you can tear the lobe that way.
Here I'm just dissecting out an inferior pulmonary ligament or station 9 lymph node.
I like to use these little glove fingertips to retrieve the lymph node specimens.
It just keeps it real clean.
And I'm starting to go anterior here and starting to dissect out the inferior pulmonary vein.
I like to get the inferior pulmonary vein dissected circumferentially.
This is a fairly big hilar lymph node or station 10.
Usually this node is kind of sandwiched between the upper and lower lobe bronchi, but this patient had a fairly generous one right on top of the inferior pulmonary vein.
But again, the first part of the procedure, the first six steps, is basically just doing the lymph node dissection.
And the more of these lobectomies I do, the more I realize the lymph node dissection is basically the whole case.
It sets everything up.
You can see here now I'm isolating the inferior pulmonary vein.
And the way I'm doing that is taking all the lymph nodes around it.
You essentially skeletonize it, get it nice and clean for division at any point later on during the case.

Once the inferior pulmonary vein is isolated, and actually just because I have pretty good exposure, you can actually see the superior pulmonary vein there, just doing a little bit of anterior dissection.

anterior.

always very consistent on the left side with the inferior pulmonary vein being the most inferior structure.

that's tethering the hilum to the posterior mediastinum.

You can do a good suprahyoid dissection.

the distinction between the basilar and suprasegmental branches and then the branches going cephalad to the upper lobe.
There you're starting to see, I'm dissecting out station 11 or 12 lymph node, which is right near the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery going to the upper lobe.
And what I'm doing here is I'm trying to see if I can get the posterior aspect of the major fissure divided.
I'm creating a little tunnel.

Basilar trunk of the pulmonary artery right on top of the lower lobe bronchus.
And there's very often a lymph node sandwiched in between the two.
And I'm dissecting that lymph node now.

This is very similar exposure to a vatsalobectomy.
But the nice thing about robotic surgery is you can expose it this way, you can expose it from behind.
Again, I'm getting all these lymph nodes out.
Lymph node dissection is so key to doing a good, clean robotic lobectomy.
And once I have the basilar trunk separated away from the lower lobe bronchus, I'll get an endolead catheter around it.
Again, I use that sponge roll sometimes to sweep, do a little blunt dissection.
Now I have a good space between the basilar trunk and the bronchus and we'll put the endolead catheter and we'll divide the basilar trunk.
Again, I'd say probably, I don't know, 80% of the time I have to divide the basilar trunk separate from the superior segmental artery.
If I can take them together in one staple fire, that's great.

I sort of retract the upper lobe away.

I keep emphasizing the lymph node dissection is key to these lobectomies.

skeletonizes everything makes everything easy to divide so again we're now dividing the superior segmental pulmonary artery and that exposes the left lower lobe bronchus well but we haven't divided the vein i usually like to divide the arterial branches before we divide the vein because i find there's less congestion less bleeding as you divide as you're dissecting the fissure but the inferior pulmonary vein is already well dissected so we lift up on the lobe and we can divide
the inferior pulmonary vein, which is already dissected nicely.
Again, I use the endolead catheter that protects the descending aorta right behind it.
